subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit you will master two of the most useful ideas in all of mathematics: fractions and percentages. Both are ways of describing part of a whole. When Amina eats half of an anjero, when Yusuf scores 85% on a test, or when Halima sees a 30% off sign at the Hargeisa market, they are all using these very ideas. Fractions and percentages are everywhere, and by the end of this unit you will handle them with real confidence.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Identify and use proper fractions, improper fractions and mixed numbers, and convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers.
Understand a fraction as division and connect it to its decimal value.
Use a fraction as an operator to find a fraction of a quantity (remembering that “of” means multiply).
Make and recognise equivalent fractions and simplify a fraction to its lowest terms.
Understand a percentage as a fraction out of 100, and calculate a percentage of an amount using several methods.
Work out percentage increases and decreases, and find an original amount after a change.
Convert freely between fractions, decimals and percentages and recognise common equivalents.
Solve multi-step word problems and interpret data given as fractions and percentages.

concepts.title: Understanding Fractions
concepts.explanation: A fraction is a way of writing part of a whole or part of a group. When Omar cuts a mango tart into 8 equal slices and eats 3 of them, he has eaten 3/8 (three-eighths) of the tart. A fraction has two parts. The numerator (top number) tells you how many parts you have. The denominator (bottom number) tells you how many equal parts the whole is divided into. The line between them means divide. So 3/4 also means 3 ÷ 4. This is one of the most important ideas in the whole unit, and we will come back to it. First, let…
concepts.example: Proper fractions, improper fractions and mixed numbers

concepts.title: A Fraction Is a Division
concepts.explanation: Every fraction is secretly a division problem. The fraction 3/4 means 3 ÷ 4, which equals 0.75 as a decimal. This one idea explains a great deal: It shows why 4/4 = 1, because 4 ÷ 4 = 1. Any fraction where the top and bottom are equal makes one whole.
concepts.example: It shows why a bigger denominator means smaller pieces. 1/8 is smaller than 1/4 because you are sharing the whole between more people, so each share is tinier.

concepts.title: Fractions as Operators – Finding a Fraction of an Amount
concepts.explanation: A fraction can also act as an operator, meaning it tells you to do something to a number. When we say “find 3/4 of 20”, the fraction 3/4 is operating on 20. In mathematics, the word “of” almost always means multiply, so “3/4 of 20” means 3/4 × 20. There are two reliable methods, and both give the same answer. Choose whichever keeps the numbers friendly.
concepts.example: Multiply first, then divide: multiply the amount by the numerator, then divide by the denominator.

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions and Simplifying
concepts.explanation: Equivalent fractions are different fractions that describe the same amount. Just as “half”, “50%” and “0.5” all name the same quantity, the fractions 1/2, 2/4, 3/6 and 4/8 are all equal. Picture half a mango tart cut into 2, 4, 6 or 8 pieces – the amount you take stays the same even though the number of pieces changes. To make an equivalent fraction, multiply the numerator and denominator by the same number:
concepts.example: To simplify (or reduce) a fraction, do the opposite: divide the top and bottom by the same number until you cannot go any further. This gives the fraction in its lowest terms. For example, 18/24: both numbers divide by…

concepts.title: Understanding Percentages
concepts.explanation: A percentage is a special fraction whose denominator is always 100. The word “per cent” literally means “per hundred” or “out of 100”. So 25% means 25 out of 100, or 25/100, which simplifies to 1/4. Percentages are useful because they give a standard way to compare. It is hard to compare 3/4 with 7/10 at a glance, but as percentages they become 75% and 70%, and now it is obvious that 3/4 is larger. Because every percentage is out of the same number, 100, comparisons become quick and fair. That is why test scores,…
concepts.example: Percentages also appear in ways you may not have noticed. A weather forecast that says there is a 30% chance of rain is really saying 30 out of every 100 similar days had rain. A pie chart of favourite sports at Nuur…

concepts.title: Calculating a Percentage of an Amount
concepts.explanation: There are three good methods for finding a percentage of an amount. Learn all three, then choose the easiest one for each problem. Since a percentage is a fraction out of 100, turn it into a simple fraction and use it as an operator. Find 20% of 150: 20% = 20/100 = 1/5, and 1/5 of 150 = 150 ÷ 5 = 30.
concepts.example: Because 10% is just divide by 10, you can build other percentages from it. Find 30% of 80: 10% of 80 = 8, and 30% = 3 × 10%, so 30% of 80 = 3 × 8 = 24. Find 15% of 200: 10% of 200 = 20, and 5% is half of that = 10, so…

methods.title: Convert an improper fraction to a mixed number
steps: Divide the numerator by the denominator.
The whole-number answer is the whole number.
The remainder becomes the new numerator.
Keep the same denominator. (11/4 = 2 3/4.)

methods.title: Convert a fraction to a percentage
steps: Divide the numerator by the denominator to get a decimal.
Multiply the decimal by 100.
Add the % symbol. (3/5 = 0.6 = 60%.)

methods.title: Simplify a fraction
steps: Find a number that divides both top and bottom.
Divide both by that number.
Repeat until no number bigger than 1 divides both. (18/24 = 3/4.)

methods.title: Percentage of an amount
steps: Solution: Build from 10%.
Then 5% is half of that = 4.
So 15% = 8 + 4 = 12 shillings.

methods.title: What percentage is one number of another?
steps: Solution: Build from 10%.
Then 5% is half of that = 4.
So 15% = 8 + 4 = 12 shillings.

methods.title: A discount problem
methods.example: A bicycle costs 150 shillings and is reduced by 20%. What is the sale price?
steps: Solution: A 20% discount means you pay 80% of the price.
(Check the long way: 20% of 150 = 30, and 150 − 30 = 120.

workedExamples.title: Convert an improper fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 17 ÷ 4 = 4 remainder 1. The whole number is 4, the remainder 1 is the new numerator, and the denominator stays 4. So 17/4 = 4 1/4.

workedExamples.title: Find a fraction of an amount
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide by the denominator, then multiply by the numerator. 18 ÷ 3 = 6, and 6 × 2 = 12 mangoes. Check: 2/3 is more than a half, and half of 18 is 9, so 12 is sensible.

workedExamples.title: Simplify a fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both 18 and 24 divide by 6. 18 ÷ 6 = 3 and 24 ÷ 6 = 4, so 18/24 = 3/4. There is no number bigger than 1 that divides both 3 and 4, so it is fully simplified.

workedExamples.title: Percentage of an amount
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Build from 10%. 10% of 80 = 8. Then 5% is half of that = 4. So 15% = 8 + 4 = 12 shillings. Worked Example 5: What percentage is one number of another? Sahra answered 16 out of 20 questions correctly. What percentage did she score? Solution: Write part over whole, then multiply by 100. (16 ÷ 20) × 100 = 0.8 × 100 = 80%.

workedExamples.title: What percentage is one number of another?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Build from 10%. 10% of 80 = 8. Then 5% is half of that = 4. So 15% = 8 + 4 = 12 shillings. Worked Example 5: What percentage is one number of another? Sahra answered 16 out of 20 questions correctly. What percentage did she score? Solution: Write part over whole, then multiply by 100. (16 ÷ 20) × 100 = 0.8 × 100 = 80%.

workedExamples.title: A discount problem
workedExamples.prompt: A bicycle costs 150 shillings and is reduced by 20%. What is the sale price?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A 20% discount means you pay 80% of the price. 80% of 150 = (80 ÷ 100) × 150 = 0.8 × 150 = 120 shillings. (Check the long way: 20% of 150 = 30, and 150 − 30 = 120. ✓)

workedExamples.title: A percentage increase
workedExamples.prompt: A young papaya tree is 20 cm tall. In one month it grows by 25%. How tall is it now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The increase is 25% of 20 = 20 ÷ 4 = 5 cm. New height = 20 + 5 = 25 cm.

workedExamples.title: Find the original amount
workedExamples.prompt: After a 60% part is measured, 60% of a number is 48. What is the number, and what is 75% of it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: If 60% of the number is 48, then 10% is 48 ÷ 6 = 8, so 100% = 8 × 10 = 80. Now 75% of 80 = (3/4) × 80 = 60.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step mixed problem
workedExamples.prompt: In a bag of 40 marbles, 1/4 are red, 30% are blue, and the rest are green. How many of each colour?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Red: 1/4 of 40 = 10. Blue: 30% of 40 = (30 ÷ 100) × 40 = 12. Green: the rest = 40 − 10 − 12 = 18. So 10 red, 12 blue, 18 green. Check: 10 + 12 + 18 = 40. ✓

workedExamples.title: What percentage is
workedExamples.prompt: What percentage is: a) 16 out of 20 b) 12 out of 48 c) 24 out of 30?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Which is larger: 3/4 of 80 or 60% of 100? Show your calculations.

activities.title: Kitchen Fraction Detective
activities.materials: a recipe (for anjero, bariis or a cake), measuring cups or spoons, paper and a pencil.
steps: Write down every fractional measurement in the recipe, such as 1/2 cup or 3/4 teaspoon.
Convert any mixed numbers (like 1 1/2 cups) into improper fractions.
Work out the amounts for half the recipe and for double the recipe.
Order all the measurements from smallest to largest to make a fraction chart.
Cooking is one of the most natural places to see fractions doing real work.

activities.title: Percentage Grid Art
activities.materials: grid paper with a 10×10 square (100 small squares), and coloured pencils.
steps: Plan a picture where each colour is a set percentage: for example 40% blue sky, 30% green grass, 20% brown trunk, 10% yellow sun.
Work out the exact number of small squares for each colour (remember, the whole grid is 100 squares).
Colour your design, keeping to your plan.
Check that all your percentages add up to 100% and all your squares add up to 100.
This shows how a percentage is simply a count out of 100.

activities.title: The Discount Shopping Game
activities.materials: old store flyers or handwritten price cards, and a pencil.
steps: Give each item a price and a percentage discount (for example 200 shillings, 25% off).
Work out the discount amount and the final price for each item.
Use the shortcut too: for 25% off, find 75% of the price in one step, and check it matches.
Compare two deals – which one truly saves more money?
You are training to be a smart shopper who can never be fooled by a sale sign.

activities.title: Sports Statistics Analyst (Investigation)
activities.materials: results from a football match or a shooting game you play, and a pencil.
steps: Record attempts and successes, for example 15 shots taken and 12 scored.
Write the success rate as a fraction, then simplify it (12/15 = 4/5).
Convert the fraction to a percentage (4/5 = 80%).
Compare two players or two matches and decide who was more accurate, explaining how you know.
This is exactly how real sports analysts compare performances fairly.

activities.title: Family Budget Pie (Group Task)
activities.materials: a pretend monthly amount (say 1,000 shillings) and paper for a circle chart.
steps: As a family, decide how to split the money by percentage: for example 40% food, 20% school, 15% transport, 15% savings, 10% charity/sadaqah.
Work out the actual amount for each part.
Draw a pie chart, making each slice roughly match its percentage of the circle.
Check that all the percentages add up to 100% and the amounts add up to the total.
Sharing wisely and giving in charity (sadaqah) can be any amount you choose – planning it with percentages helps the whole family see the picture.

activities.title: Conversion Snap
activities.materials: small cards. On each, write a fraction, a decimal or a percentage.
steps: Make matching sets, such as 1/2, 0.5 and 50%, for several common values.
Shuffle all the cards and deal them out between two players.
Take turns laying a card face up; shout “Snap!” when two cards on the table are equal in value.
The player who spots the most correct matches wins.
Playing this a few times will fix the common equivalents firmly in your memory.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Fraction of an Amount “Of” means multiply. Divide by the denominator, then multiply by the numerator. 3/4 of 20 = (20 ÷ 4) × 3 = 15.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Percentage of an Amount A percentage is a fraction out of 100. Method: (amount × percentage) ÷ 100. 35% of 120 = (120 × 35) ÷ 100 = 42.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Increase and Decrease For a discount of 25%, you pay 75% of the price. For an increase of 10%, the new price is 110% of the old. To find the original, divide by the new percentage as a decimal.

terms: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A whole number with a proper fraction
Reduce a fraction to lowest terms
An increase or decrease given as a percentage
commonMistakes: Second discount off the first price
Take each discount off the latest price

realProblems.prompt: At the Kismaayo market, Sagal buys a basket priced at 120 shillings with a 25% discount. How much does she pay?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Maryam has saved 45 shillings towards a goal of 120 shillings. What fraction of her goal has she reached, and what percentage is this?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A survey of 150 people found that 2/5 prefer tea, 40% prefer coffee, and the rest prefer camel milk. How many prefer each drink?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A pupil's grade is homework (30%), quizzes (25%) and tests (45%). She scores 85% in homework, 78% in quizzes and 92% in tests. What is her overall grade?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A town of 8,000 people has 3/8 children and 45% adults, and the rest are seniors. How many people are in each group?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Abdi invests 500 shillings. After one year it increases by 8%. How much does he have now?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Which is larger: 3/4 of 80 or 60% of 100? Show your calculations.
A store buys an item for 50 shillings and sells it for 65 shillings. What percentage profit does the store make?
Order from smallest to largest: 5/8, 62%, 0.65, 3/5. Convert them to the same form to compare.
If 60% of a number is 48, what is 75% of the same number?
A shirt costs 45 shillings. There is a 20% discount, then a further 10% off the reduced price. What is the final price?
Explain the key idea in Calculating a Percentage of an Amount.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: There are three good methods for finding a percentage of an amount. Learn all three, then choose the easiest one for each problem. Since a percentage is a fraction out of 100, turn it into a simple fraction and use it as an operator. Find 20% of 150: 20% = 20/100 = 1/5, and 1/5 of 150 = 150 ÷ 5 = 30.

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

questions.question: What does “Numerator / Denominator” mean?
options: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A whole number with a proper fraction
questions.answer: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerator / Denominator means Top number / bottom number of a fraction.

questions.question: What does “Proper fraction” mean?
options: A whole number with a proper fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Proper fraction means Less than one whole (top < bottom).

questions.question: What does “Improper fraction” mean?
options: A whole number with a proper fraction
Reduce a fraction to lowest terms
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Improper fraction means One whole or more (top ≥ bottom).

rounds.explanation: Numerator / Denominator means Top number / bottom number of a fraction.

selfAssessment: I can identify and use proper fractions, improper fractions and mixed numbers, and convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers.
I can understand a fraction as division and connect it to its decimal value.
I can use a fraction as an operator to find a fraction of a quantity (remembering that “of” means multiply).
I can make and recognise equivalent fractions and simplify a fraction to its lowest terms.
I can understand a percentage as a fraction out of 100, and calculate a percentage of an amount using several methods.
I can work out percentage increases and decreases, and find an original amount after a change.
I can convert freely between fractions, decimals and percentages and recognise common equivalents.
I can solve multi-step word problems and interpret data given as fractions and percentages.
